original_abstract: Highly active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are required for the development of photoelectrochemical devices that generate hydrogen efficiently from water using solar energy. Here, we identify the origin of a 500-fold OER activity enhancement that can be achieved with mixed (Ni,Fe)oxyhydroxides (Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH) over their pure Ni and Fe parent compounds, resulting in one of the most active currently known OER catalysts in alkaline electrolyte. Operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) using high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) reveals that Fe(3+) in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH occupies octahedral sites with unusually short Fe-O bond distances, induced by edge-sharing with surrounding [NiO6] octahedra. Using computational methods, we establish that this structural motif results in near optimal adsorption energies of OER intermediates and low overpotentials at Fe sites. By contrast, Ni sites in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH are not active sites for the oxidation of water.
humanized_abstract: Active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are needed for solar driven hydrogen evolution photoelectrochemical devices using water as the sacrificial resource. We have determined the cause for an order of magnitude increase in OER activity for mixed (Ni,Fe)oxyhydroxides—500 times greater than that of their pure parent compounds—making this catalyst one of the most active ever reported in alkaline solution. Using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) at the Ni K and Fe K edges collected with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) on catalyst electrodes that are acting during OER and also after several hours of operation we have elucidated the chemical properties of Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH catalysts that are responsible for their marked improvements over those of the parent pure metal compounds. Our data reveal that the Fe3+ ion resides at the octahedral site of Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH and has shortened Fe-O bonds relative to those of homogeneous FeOOH which is a consequence of the Ni-oxide shared edge with an adjacent [NiO6] site. Modeling of simulation studies of OER for this mixed metal catalyst, we confirm the edge-sharing between the iron and nickel sites in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH, leads to a nearly optimal adsorption energy of OER-reactions intermediates at the iron centers along with low overpotentials. Additionally, we found that nickel in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH does not act as an active site for OER.

Our data reveal that the Fe3+ ion resides at the octahedral site of Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH and has shortened Fe-O bonds relative to those of homogeneous FeOOH which is a consequence of the Ni-oxide shared edge with an adjacent [NiO6] site.
